title: A Review of LEO-Satellite Communication Payloads for Integrated Communication, Navigation, and Remote Sensing: Opportunities, Challenges, Future Directions
authors: Mingming Hui, Shenghua Zhai, Daqing Wang, et al.
abstract: The miniaturization of satellite payloads and the reduction in launch costs have driven the rapid development of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites in the fields of communication, navigation, and remote sensing (CNS), reshaping the industry and global information infrastructure. However, there remain significant challenges and obstacles in the application of LEO satellites within these domains. This article aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of LEO satellites, with particular emphasis on the development and challenges of communication payloads, to promote further research and applications in these areas. First, this article examines the evolution of LEO systems and communication payload capabilities from different historical perspectives and user demands. It then explores the opportunities for LEO satellites in the context of global communication infrastructure, particularly in enhancing global connectivity, supporting intelligent transportation through high-precision positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT), and advancing environmental monitoring via passive radar (PR) in remote sensing applications. By systematically reviewing the literature on network architectures, medium access control, physical-layer technologies, constellation designs, interference management, the application of artificial intelligence, and cost control strategies, this study identifies the major challenges these technologies face in CNS applications. Additionally, this article discusses future research directions, including large-scale constellations, multifunctional platform integration, advances in intelligence and automation, network convergence and interoperability, interference mitigation, and sustainability. These areas are explored to provide pathways for further technological advancements.

title: 2016 Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Coccidioidomycosis
authors: John N. Galgiani, Neil M. Ampel, Janis E. Blair, et al.
abstract: It is important to realize that guidelines cannot always account for individual variation among patients. They are not intended to supplant physician judgment with respect to particular patients or special clinical situations. Infectious Diseases Society of America considers adherence to these guidelines to be voluntary, with the ultimate determination regarding their application to be made by the physician in the light of each patient's individual circumstances.Coccidioidomycosis, also known as San Joaquin Valley fever, is a systemic infection endemic to parts of the southwestern United States and elsewhere in the Western Hemisphere. Residence in and recent travel to these areas are critical elements for the accurate recognition of patients who develop this infection. In this practice guideline, we have organized our recommendations to address actionable questions concerning the entire spectrum of clinical syndromes. These can range from initial pulmonary infection, which eventually resolves whether or not antifungal therapy is administered, to a variety of pulmonary and extrapulmonary complications. Additional recommendations address management of coccidioidomycosis occurring for special at-risk populations. Finally, preemptive management strategies are outlined in certain at-risk populations and after unintentional laboratory exposure.

title: Randomized, double-blind trial of fluconazole versus voriconazole for prevention of invasive fungal infection after allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation
authors: John R. Wingard, Shelly L. Carter, Thomas J. Walsh, et al.
abstract: Invasive fungal infection (IFI) is a serious threat after allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT). This multicenter, randomized, double-blind trial compared fluconazole (N = 295) versus voriconazole (N = 305) for the prevention of IFI in the context of a structured fungal screening program. Patients undergoing myeloablative allogeneic HCT were randomized before HCT to receive study drugs for 100 days, or for 180 days in higher-risk patients. Serum galactomannan was assayed twice weekly for 60 days, then at least weekly until day 100. Positive galactomannan or suggestive signs triggered mandatory evaluation for IFI. The primary endpoint was freedom from IFI or death (fungal-free survival; FFS) at 180 days. Despite trends to fewer IFIs (7.3% vs 11.2%; P = .12), Aspergillus infections (9 vs 17; P = .09), and less frequent empiric antifungal therapy (24.1% vs 30.2%, P = .11) with voriconazole, FFS rates (75% vs 78%; P = .49) at 180 days were similar with fluconazole and voriconazole, respectively. Relapse-free and overall survival and the incidence of severe adverse events were also similar. This study demonstrates that in the context of intensive monitoring and structured empiric antifungal therapy, 6-month FFS and overall survival did not differ in allogeneic HCT recipients given prophylactic fluconazole or voriconazole. This trial was registered at www.clinicaltrials.gov as NCT00075803.

title: Proceedings of the International Symposium
authors: Dominique A. Vuitton, Laurence Millon, Bruno Gottstein, et al.
abstract: Background: To correlate the appearance of Alveolar Echinococcosis (AE) hepatic lesions in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) as defined by Kodama, to the metabolic activity visualized in 18-Fluoro-DeoxyGlucose Positron Emission Tomography combined with Computed Tomography (PET/CT). Methods: Forty-two patients (25 men; mean age: 62.2) diagnosed with AE and who underwent both MRI and PET/CT were included. Three independent readers blinded with regard to the PET/CT information, divided the forty-two hepatic lesions into five types according to Kodama's classification. Concerning PET/CT, two independent readers, unaware of the MRI information, considered the results as positive when an increased FDG-uptake was observed at 1 or 3 hours after FDG injection, and as negative when no increased uptake was noted. Inter-observer agreement was assessed by using j statistics. Results: Forty-two lesions were counted and the mean diameter of overall evaluated lesions was 6.3 cm. One lesion (2.4%) was categorized as type 1, 11 (26.2%) as type 2, 24 (57.1%) as type 3, 3 (7.1%) as type 4 and 3 (7.1%) as type 5. The inter-observer analysis found a j coefficient of 0.96. All type-1, 90.9% of type-2 and 87.5% of type-3 lesions showed an increased FDG-uptake on PET/CT images. All non-microcystic AE liver lesions (types 4, 5) showed no abnormal increased FDG uptake on PET/CT images. The inter-observer analysis at one and three hours found a j coefficient of: 0.95 and 0.92, respectively. Conclusion: In patients with AE liver lesions, the absence of micro-cysts on MRI is strongly correlated to a metabolically inactive disease.

title: Molecular targeted therapy for anticancer treatment
authors: Hye‐Young Min, Ho‐Young Lee
abstract: Since the initial clinical approval in the late 1990s and remarkable anticancer effects for certain types of cancer, molecular targeted therapy utilizing small molecule agents or therapeutic monoclonal antibodies acting as signal transduction inhibitors has served as a fundamental backbone in precision medicine for cancer treatment. These approaches are now used clinically as first-line therapy for various types of human cancers. Compared to conventional chemotherapy, targeted therapeutic agents have efficient anticancer effects with fewer side effects. However, the emergence of drug resistance is a major drawback of molecular targeted therapy, and several strategies have been attempted to improve therapeutic efficacy by overcoming such resistance. Herein, we summarize current knowledge regarding several targeted therapeutic agents, including classification, a brief biology of target kinases, mechanisms of action, examples of clinically used targeted therapy, and perspectives for future development.

title: Case Definitions, Diagnostic Algorithms, and Priorities in Encephalitis: Consensus Statement of the International Encephalitis Consortium
authors: Arun Venkatesan, Allan R. Tunkel, Karen C. Bloch, et al.
abstract: We anticipate that this document, representing a synthesis of our discussions and supported by literature, will serve as a practical aid to clinicians evaluating patients with suspected encephalitis and will identify key areas and approaches to advance our knowledge of encephalitis.

title: MoS<sub>2</sub>-Coated MOF-Derived Hollow Heterostructures for Electromagnetic Wave Absorption
authors: Yue Sun, Yanxiang Wang, Dongming Liu, et al.
abstract: Structural design constitutes one of the crucial approaches for augmenting the wave-absorbing capacity of electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorbers, and the incorporation of cavity structures represents a typical methodology therein. In this work, the MoS<sub>2</sub>-coated metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived Hollow-MoS<sub>2</sub>@CNS@CoS<sub>2</sub> composite materials (HCNSs) were prepared by combining tannic acid-protected etching, carbonization, and hydrothermal methods. Especially, HCNS700, which possessed both a hollow structure and a layered heterogeneous structure, demonstrated excellent EMW absorption properties. It attained an optimal reflection loss of -63.63 dB at 16.4 GHz and -58.97 dB at 10.4 GHz, along with an extremely low thickness. In addition, the radar cross section simulation demonstrated that HCNS700 possessed excellent electromagnetic stealth capabilities. Its excellent performance is put down to the multiple loss mechanisms brought by the special structure, including multiple scattering of EMW caused by the hollow structure, interface polarization caused by the heterogeneous interfaces of MoS<sub>2</sub>, CoS<sub>2</sub>, and the carbon matrix, dipole polarization caused by element doping and defects, and optimization of impedance matching by MoS<sub>2</sub>. This research offers a novel concept for the design of EMW-absorbing materials with hollow heterogeneous layered structures.

title: Smouldering multiple sclerosis: the ‘real MS’
authors: Gavin Giovannoni, Veronica Popescu, Jens Wuerfel, et al.
abstract: Using a philosophical approach or deductive reasoning, we challenge the dominant clinico-radiological worldview that defines multiple sclerosis (MS) as a focal inflammatory disease of the central nervous system (CNS). We provide a range of evidence to argue that the 'real MS' is in fact driven primarily by a smouldering pathological disease process. In natural history studies and clinical trials, relapses and focal activity revealed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in MS patients on placebo or on disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) were found to be poor predictors of long-term disease evolution and were dissociated from disability outcomes. In addition, the progressive accumulation of disability in MS can occur independently of relapse activity from early in the disease course. This scenario is underpinned by a more diffuse smouldering pathological process that may affect the entire CNS. Many putative pathological drivers of smouldering MS can be potentially modified by specific therapeutic strategies, an approach that may have major implications for the management of MS patients. We hypothesise that therapeutically targeting a state of 'no evident inflammatory disease activity' (NEIDA) cannot sufficiently prevent disability accumulation in MS, meaning that treatment should also focus on other brain and spinal cord pathological processes contributing to the slow loss of neurological function. This should also be complemented with a holistic approach to the management of other systemic disease processes that have been shown to worsen MS outcomes.

title: Slow expansion of multiple sclerosis iron rim lesions: pathology and 7 T magnetic resonance imaging
authors: Assunta Dal‐Bianco, Günther Grabner, Claudia Kronnerwetter, et al.
abstract: In multiple sclerosis (MS), iron accumulates inside activated microglia/macrophages at edges of some chronic demyelinated lesions, forming rims. In susceptibility-based magnetic resonance imaging at 7 T, iron-laden microglia/macrophages induce a rim of decreased signal at lesion edges and have been associated with slowly expanding lesions. We aimed to determine (1) what lesion types and stages are associated with iron accumulation at their edges, (2) what cells at the lesion edges accumulate iron and what is their activation status, (3) how reliably can iron accumulation at the lesion edge be detected by 7 T magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and (4) if lesions with rims enlarge over time in vivo, when compared to lesions without rims. Double-hemispheric brain sections of 28 MS cases were stained for iron, myelin, and microglia/macrophages. Prior to histology, 4 of these 28 cases were imaged at 7 T using post-mortem susceptibility-weighted imaging. In vivo, seven MS patients underwent annual neurological examinations and 7 T MRI for 3.5 years, using a fluid attenuated inversion recovery/susceptibility-weighted imaging fusion sequence. Pathologically, we found iron rims around slowly expanding and some inactive lesions but hardly around remyelinated shadow plaques. Iron in rims was mainly present in microglia/macrophages with a pro-inflammatory activation status, but only very rarely in astrocytes. Histological validation of post-mortem susceptibility-weighted imaging revealed a quantitative threshold of iron-laden microglia when a rim was visible. Slowly expanding lesions significantly exceeded this threshold, when compared with inactive lesions (p = 0.003). We show for the first time that rim lesions significantly expanded in vivo after 3.5 years, compared to lesions without rims (p = 0.003). Thus, slow expansion of MS lesions with rims, which reflects chronic lesion activity, may, in the future, become an MRI marker for disease activity in MS.

title: The Second ASRA Practice Advisory on Neurologic Complications Associated With Regional Anesthesia and Pain Medicine
authors: Joseph M. Neal, Michael J. Barrington, Richard Brull, et al.
abstract: The Second American Society of Regional Anesthesia and Pain Medicine Practice Advisory on Neurologic Complications Associated With Regional Anesthesia and Pain Medicine updates information that was originally presented at the Society's first open forum on this subject (2005) and published in 2008. Portions of the second advisory were presented in an open forum (2012) and are herein updated, with attention to those topics subject to evolving knowledge since the first and second advisory conferences. The second advisory briefly summarizes recommendations that have not changed substantially. New to this iteration of the advisory is information related to the risk of nerve injury inherent to common orthopedic surgical procedures. Recommendations are expanded regarding the preventive role of various monitoring technologies such as ultrasound guidance and injection pressure monitoring. New clinical recommendations focus on emerging concerns including spinal stenosis and vertebral canal pathologies, blood pressure management during neuraxial anesthesia, administering blocks in anesthetized or deeply sedated patients, patients with preexisting neurologic disease, and inflammatory neuropathies. An updated diagnostic and treatment algorithm is presented.

title: Malaria Disease Recommendations for Solid Organ Transplant Recipients and Donors
authors: Lı́gia Camera Pierrotti, Marilyn Eckstein Levi, Sílvia Maria Di Santi, et al.
abstract: INTRODUCTION AND EPIDEMIOLOGY Malaria is an acute febrile infection caused by 5 species of Plasmodium (P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae, and P. knowlesi). A life-threatening illness, malaria is regarded as the most important human parasitic disease. Over 40% of the world’s population lives in tropical areas of sub-Saharan Africa, Asia, Oceania, and Latin America where malaria is transmitted. Globally, 214 million new cases of malaria were estimated in 2015 with 438 000 deaths,1 with Latin America accounting for about 10% of total malaria cases.2 About 90% of malaria cases in the Americas occur in the Amazon basin shared by Brazil, Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, French Guiana, Peru, Suriname, and Venezuela, whereas the other 10% occur outside Amazon regions, mainly along the coastal areas and lowland Andean valleys, as well as in some North and Central American countries, from Mexico to Panama2 (Figure 1). Brazil is responsible for 60% of the cases and for half of the deaths due to malaria in the region.2 Since 2000, substantial decreases in malaria incidence have been observed in the World Health Organization (WHO) Region of the Americas, and several countries are in preelimination and elimination phases, including Argentina, Belize, Costa Rica, Ecuador, El Salvador, Mexico, and Paraguay.1FIGURE 1: Confirmed malaria cases per 1000 population, 2013. Source: WHO Malaria Report 2014.3Although only about 8% of estimated global cases are due to P. vivax infections,3 in Latin American countries, this plasmodial species accounts for about 75% of malaria cases; the remaining 25% are caused by P. falciparum, and less than 0.1% by P. malariae.2 Malaria is typically transmitted to humans by the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes. Less commonly, malaria may also be acquired by blood transfusions, sharing contaminated needles and syringes, by means of mother-to-child transmission, or after solid-organ transplantation (SOT).4 Posttransplant malaria has been rarely reported in the literature even in endemic areas.5,6 One potential reason for this uncommon complication among SOT recipients is that areas of malaria risk generally have very low transplant activity. Brazil, for instance, is the country that accounts for the second largest absolute number of kidney and liver transplant procedures in the world.7 Amazon basin, where 99.8% of Brazilian malaria cases occur, was, however, responsible for less than 2% of the national supply of organs and transplant procedures in 2015.7 In the reported malaria infections associated with transplants, the source of infection has not always been possible to define. The infection can be a consequence of a donor-derived infection (DDI), or a reactivation of previous infection in the recipient (related to relapses in the case of P. vivax and P. ovale because of the reactivation of dormant hypnozoites in the liver or recrudescence because of persistent parasite in the blood despite immune response or treatment), or de novo posttransplant infection by the bite of Anopheles spp mosquitoes in endemic settings. DDI has been reported through transfusion of blood and blood products, through red blood cells (RBCs) within the transplanted organ, and through the transplanted liver from donors who were infected by P. vivax and P. ovale, because of the persistence of hypnozoites within hepatocytes when infection is due to either of these plasmodial species.4 Most malaria cases reported among SOT recipients have been described after kidney transplant. The potential of DDI of malaria are summarized in Table 1.TABLE 1: Malaria cases reported in SOT recipients probably through infected graft or blood transfusions posttransplantCLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS The malaria parasite life cycle involves 2 hosts and is illustrated in Figure 2.8 When the infected female of Anopheles mosquito takes a blood meal to enable the maturation of the eggs, sporozoites are injected to the dermis and start a human cycle. After remaining for up to 6 hours in the dermis, some of the sporozoites cross the capillary endothelium, reach the bloodstream, and infect hepatocytes. Inside liver cells, asexual reproduction begins, named schizogony, which results in the formation of liver schizonts, that release the merozoite to the bloodstream.FIGURE 2: Biological cycle of the parasite in human malaria. Source from Zhang et al.8At the end of the initial asexual replication in the liver (the preerythrocytic phase), thousands of unicelular parasitic forms, the merozoites, are released to the bloodstream, thus initiating the erytrhocyte cycle. P. vivax and P. ovale can generate dormant stage parasites (hypnozoites) that persist in the hepatocytes and may resume the replication after a variable period (weeks, or even years later). The hypozoites reactivation accounts for the reappearance of symptoms in relapses. In the blood, the released merozoites infect RBCs and initiate a new asexual schizogonic reproduction (erythrocytic cycle). The ring stage trophozoites mature into schizonts, rupture RBCs, and release merozoites that invade other erytrhocytes. Some parasites differentiate into sexual erytrhocytic stages (gametocytes), which are the forms that will infect the anopheline vector (vector cycle). It is important to highlight that the preerythrocytic cycle is asymptomatic and that clinical disease starts after infected RBCs periodically release merozoites and induce fever. Symptoms of uncomplicated malaria are nonspecific and indistinguishable from viral, bacterial, and other parasitic infections, including fever, chills, malaise, myalgia, arthralgia, and headache. Malaria symptoms appear 7 to 30 days after the mosquito bite.9 The duration of hepatic schizogony determines variation in the incubation period of malaria; shorter periods are observed with P. falciparum and P. vivax and the longer periods with P. malariae. However, longer incubation periods up to 3 months from possible exposure may occur and should be investigated immediately to permit prompt treatment. Prophylaxis with antimalarial drugs can delay the appearance of malaria symptoms by weeks or even months after the traveler has left endemic areas.9 If untreated, the disease can lead to severe complications and death. The severity of the disease is related to the type of transplantation, Plasmodium species, intensity of parasitemia, immunosuppression status, and delays in diagnosis and in onset of antimalarial therapy.10-12P. falciparum and P. knowlesi release a higher number of merozoites from the liver. Therefore, they are able to infect a large number of RBCs and can cause rapidly progressive severe illness, with the highest rates of complications and mortality; whereas P. vivax, P. ovale, and P. malariae, are associated with lower parasitemia and are less likely to cause severe manifestations. Severe disease is characterized by 1 or more of the following clinical and laboratory criteria: impaired consciousness or coma, severe normocytic anemia (hemoglobin, < 7 g/dL), thrombocytopenia, renal failure, acute respiratory distress syndrome, hypotension, disseminated intravascular coagulation, spontaneous bleeding, acidosis (elevated lactate), hemoglobinuria, jaundice, repetead generalized seizures, and/or level of parasitemia according to the patient immune response. A parasitemia of 2% or more of the RBC in nonimmune individuals and 5% or more RBC in semi-immunes are classified as severe malaria.13 Posttransplant malaria has not been necessarily associated with poor outcomes when compared with infections in the general population.12 Immunossupression, however, may be associated with fatality. A previous case of of P. malariae infection was reported in an asplenic patient from Brazil who acquired the parasite from blood transfusion during surgery, even though P. malariae is usually characterized by a benign disease in the general population.14 DIAGNOSIS Different diagnostic approaches should be taken into account for malaria: point-of-care diagnosis for symptomatic infections and the challenge of detecting parasites in asymptomatic infections, characterized by submicroscopic parasitemia. In the context of SOT, the focus must rely on sensitive techniques for pretransplant malaria screening aiming at the detection of low-level parasitemia. The Giemsa-stained thick blood smear microscopy remains the gold standard for malaria diagnosis, even though this technique is unchanged because it was introduced more than a hundred years ago. Conventional routine microscopy presents a limit of detection between 50 and 500 parasites/μL.15,16 Nonetheless, microscopy enables identification of individual species and developmental stages and provides a quantitative tool for monitoring response to treatment and detecting relapse. Detection of lower parasitemia (down to 5 parasites/μL) has been reported in some studies performed by very well-trained personnel.17,18 However, in individuals with low parasitemia or mixed infections, the accuracy of diagnosis can be challenging even to expert microscopists.19 Alternatively, rapid diagnostic test (RDT) is based on the identification of plasmodial antigens using immunochromatography. In some cases, detection is species-specific, such as P. falciparum histidine-rich protein (PfHRP) 2; in others, detection is based on pan-Plasmodium lactate dehydrogenase (pan-pLDH), P. vivax-specific and P. falciparum-specific pLDH or genus-specific aldolase (pan-aldolase).20 The specificity and sensitivity of these tests vary considerably according to the comercially available RDT used and the Plasmodium species (sensitivity is higher for P. falciparum and lower for all other species).21 However, among 200 RDTs products evaluated by the WHO, the limit of detection was 200 parasites/μL.22 Other disadvantages are that the test cannot quantify parasitemia; therefore, it is not useful to monitor therapy, exhibits cross-reactivity against human rheumatoid fator22 and false-negative results due to deletions of PfHRP2 and PfHRP3 genes.23 In addition, the higher reatogenicity and stability of HRP2 confer higher sensitivity to this test compared with lactate dehydrogenase and aldolase, but may yield false-positive results because of the persistence of PfHRP2 in the peripheral blood after treatment. Because of their performance and limitations, RDTs are considered useful in remote endemic areas where rapid diagnosis of malaria is needed but a skilled personnel to perform the gold standard microscopy may not be available.21 After the advent of molecular tools for malaria diagnosis, several protocols were reported, mainly targeting 18S ribosomal RNA gene sequences, detecting 0.05 to 5 parasites/μL.24 Snounou and colleagues25 developed a very sensitive nested PCR, able to differentiate between P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. malariae, and P. ovale. However, this technique is laborious and time-consuming. Genus-specific real-time PCR with limit of detection of 0.35 parasites/μL26 is an alternative for screening blood samples. Higher sensitivity can be achieved using larger volumes of blood, increasing the limit of detection to 0.022 arasites/μL of blood.27 The primers used for genomic molecular detecting tests can be common to all Plasmodium species (panspecific) or specie specific. Serologic tests are not suitable for the diagnosis of the malaria disease but rather measures past exposure, because antibodies can be detected even after cure, remaining in high titers even for years, particularly in patients repeatedly exposed to reinfections.28 RISK ASSESSMENT Donors and candidates who are at risk for malaria infection are divided into those who were born or raised in endemic areas and travelers.29 They may be apparently healthy and/or may also have been exposed to malaria in the distant past. The most important screening tool is therefore epidemiological; if history suggests that there is malaria risk or is unclear, laboratory confirmation should be obtained.30,31 Epidemiological History An epidemiological history relevant for malaria for both donor and candidate must be routinely investigated as outlined in Table 2.TABLE 2: Epidemiological history for malaria risk evaluation41,69,71Screening for Asymptomatic Parasitemia Residents of malarious areas are subjected to repeated exposures, resulting in a semi-immune status, often insufficient to achieve long-term or protective immunity.32 As a result, asymptomatic infections with low numbers of circulating parasites may develop.33 If they relocate to a nonendemic area they often resolve their infections, usually in 2 years for P. falciparum and in 3 years for P. vivax or P. ovale, whereas P. malariae infections persist indefinitely. Screening testing possibilities include thick blood smear (less sensitive), RDT for plasmodial antigens detection, NAT, nucleic acid testing (NAT) and antimalarial antibody detection.30 Screening for Plasmodium parasitemia with microscopy in individuals who have had previous malaria exposure is not sensitive enough, because donors who have transmitted the infection typically present with low-level parasitemias that may not be detected even by careful examination. Likewise, RDTs are not useful for detecting low parasitemia.15 Therefore, the best screening option in this scenario is the detection of Plasmodium DNA or RNA by the PCR, which is more sensitive and specific than microscopy, and has been used in donor blood screening.34 Those techniques allow detection of low parasitemia among donors even years after previous exposure because of the possibility of persistent asymptomatic infection.35 Serologic tests could also be used, but detection of antibodies does not necessarily indicate the presence of parasitemia but rather indicate exposure36 with the risk of organ exclusion of some donors with no parasitemia, as noted in European countries where serology testing was used to screen donors who have traveled to malarious areas.29,36 Serology testing, however, may be considered if the donor exposure history is unclear.6 The Role of Hypnozoites in Liver Transplantation The presence of a latent hepatic phase in P. vivax and P. ovale infections is a particular concern for donor screening before liver transplantation. After successful treatment of primary infection, parasites reside in hepatocytes in a dormant stage as hypnozoites, resulting in relapses after several months or even years.37,38 Pretransplant screening of blood smears will not identify hypnozoites, because these infect liver cells only. As a result, these 2 plasmodial species transmission through the tranplanted liver has been reported.6,39 The possibility of these 2 plasmodial species transmission through the SOT other than liver has also been reported5; authors point out that surgical stress can trigger malaria relapse. MANAGEMENT OF SYMPTOMATIC PARASITEMIA All potential donors with evidence of active infections are deferred from donation until diagnosed and treated.
